subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The chapter is entirely about the arithmetic of negative quantities — it builds multiplication and division of integers from the token model, establishes the sign rules, and explicitly presents Brahmagupta's rules for operating on fortunes and debts, which is exactly the inclusion of zero and negative quantities as numbers and the operations on them.

Section 10.2 investigates the properties of the integers as a number set — commutativity, associativity, and distributivity — and section 10.1 visualises integers on the number line through the carrom-coin model, so students explore the set of integers and their properties, an independent strand from carrying out the operations themselves.

adjunct_competencies.justification: The chapter devotes a full passage to Brahmagupta's Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta (628 CE), quoting his fortune (dhana) and debt (ṛṇa) rules for the product and quotient of positive and negative numbers and noting this as the first articulation of such rules — a direct appreciation of an Indian mathematician's contribution.

The sign rules are not asserted but discovered inductively from multiplication patterns and the times-tables, then justified, and students are asked to generalise the sign of a product of many integers and to argue why commutativity, associativity, and distributivity hold — a sustained inductive-to-deductive reasoning thread.

The chapter is threaded with puzzles and games inviting students to find their own solutions — Rakesh's sum-and-difference number game, the invariant magic grid, the alien 'pibs' currency problem, the maximum/minimum expression task, and the fill-in-the-blanks-five-ways problems — culminating in the traditional game Terhüchü.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to multiply and divide integers and evaluate integer expressions by using token models and multiplication patterns to justify the sign rules and the commutative, associative, and distributive properties.
